\section{Introduction}

Light field imaging captures both spatial and angular radiance variations, providing dense geometric constraints for high-precision depth estimation \cite{ng2005, ng2006}. By recording the light field as a 4D function, plenoptic cameras enable continuous viewpoint variations and robust 3D surface reconstruction, capabilities that are essential for autonomous navigation, augmented reality, and industrial inspection \cite{honauer2016}. Despite the theoretical advantages of capturing complete light transport data, practical depth estimation remains exceptionally challenging when venturing beyond controlled laboratory environments into real-world scenes dominated by complex material properties and geometry \cite{eigen2014, chen2017}.

The primary obstacle stems from the pervasive presence of non-Lambertian surfaces—such as glass, polished metal, and glossy ceramics—whose view-dependent reflectance violates the global Lambertian assumption \cite{blinn1977}. Mainstream light field depth estimation relies heavily on the Epipolar Plane Image (EPI) paradigm, extracting depth by analyzing 2D slope variations along specific directional slices \cite{wanner2014, heber2018}. However, EPI-based architectures inherently process only four directional slices, utilizing a mere 34.6\% of the available $9 \times 9 = 81$ angular samples. This rigid 2D slicing discards over 65\% of the high-dimensional angular distribution, stripping away the essential physical multi-view evidence required to decode specular reflections and sub-surface scattering.

This structural information loss imposes an unbreakable accuracy ceiling and leads to catastrophic failures in non-Lambertian regions. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that state-of-the-art EPI-based networks \cite{lyu2022, peng2022} exhibit severe texture-copying artifacts on specular surfaces, where the predicted depth mimics the underlying 2D image texture rather than the actual 3D geometry. On standard non-Lambertian benchmarks, EPI-based methods structurally fail to decouple reflectance from geometry, with the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) surging to 0.411, compared to an impressive 0.081 in mixed Lambertian scenes. No amount of EPI network refinement can overcome the theoretical MAE lower bound of 0.28 for non-Lambertian materials, as the root cause lies in the mathematical mismatch between the directional EPI formulation and the physics of non-Lambertian light transport \cite{li2021, zhang2023}.

Addressing this bottleneck requires abandoning the rigid EPI formulation and moving toward a holistic, physics-driven framework that utilizes the full angular domain. Existing approaches attempt to mitigate angular noise or occlusions \cite{wang2023}, yet they fail to explicitly model the physical light-matter interactions causing specular distortion. There is an urgent necessity to establish a physical mapping between light field angular frequencies and material properties, transforming the naturally flat cost volumes—traditionally viewed as a failure mode in specular regions—into explicit uncertainty signals for depth propagation. Overcoming these physical and architectural limitations is critical for deploying robust 3D vision systems in complex, uncontrolled environments.

Traditional light field depth estimation methods predominantly rely on explicit geometric constraints, such as epipolar plane image (EPI) (EPI) analysis and defocus metrics. Methodologically, these algorithms universally assume globally Lambertian reflectance, treating view-dependent specularity as local noise \cite{wanner2014}. Technically, depth is derived by analyzing 2D slopes along fixed directional EPI slices, which discards the high-dimensional angular distribution. This structural bottleneck restricts the spatial aperture to a marginal fraction of the available viewpoints, stripping away the multi-view evidence required to decode specular and sub-surface scattering phenomena \cite{tao2013}. Consequently, geometric matching fails completely when the local bidirectional reflectance distribution function (BRDF) deviates from ideal diffuse reflection, producing persistent outliers in regions containing glass or polished metal \cite{blinn1977}.

Early deep learning frameworks integrated convolutional neural networks with the EPI paradigm to learn implicit structural mappings from 2D slices \cite{heber2018}. Methodologically, these data-driven models bypass explicit physical constraints, treating the 4D light field as generic 2D textures without modeling the underlying light transport. Technically, they inherit the rigid directional slicing limitation, utilizing a mere 34.6\% of the available angular samples. Because the receptive fields are strictly bounded by these independent 2D slices, the networks lack multi-scale context aggregation across the global aperture. This architectural blindness prevents the model from distinguishing true depth edges from specular reflections, inevitably leading to severe texture-copying artifacts where the predicted depth mimics the underlying image texture \cite{lyu2022}.

Recent state-of-the-art approaches attempt to mitigate non-Lambertian interference via sophisticated attention mechanisms or multi-view feature fusion \cite{li2021, peng2022}. Methodologically, they formulate non-Lambertian regions as statistical outliers to be suppressed, rather than physically modeling the material properties. Technically, these architectures compute matching costs or energy metrics using fixed global parameters, failing to adapt to abrupt material transitions. Without an explicit mechanism to differentiate between rough Lambertian, smooth Lambertian, and specular regions, the extracted depth features suffer from severe signal interference. Smooth Lambertian pseudo-textures artificially mask authentic high-frequency depth discontinuities, degrading the signal-to-noise ratio of the constructed cost volumes \cite{zhang2023}.

These persistent limitations across existing paradigms expose a critical gap: purely geometric slicing or unconstrained data-driven mapping cannot resolve the fundamental ambiguities of non-Lambertian reflectance. Therefore, a method that explicitly decouples reflectance components via physical modeling, leverages full-aperture angular geometry, and implements adaptive energy adjustment is urgently needed to advance robust light field depth estimation.

In this paper, we propose a unified dual-mask physical model for non-Lambertian light field depth estimation. Departing from rigid directional EPI slicing and globally diffuse assumptions, we formulate the depth estimation problem by explicitly modeling the underlying light transport. The proposed framework physically quantifies pixel-level roughness and deflection vector fields, integrating this physical prior with a Multi-scale Angular Cost Volume Network (MACVO-Net). This network shifts the feature extraction paradigm from implicit 2D slice processing to explicit 4D cost volume construction, utilizing all available angular views. By decoupling material reflectance properties from multi-view geometric correspondence, the framework robustly resolves depth ambiguity caused by view-dependent specularity and sub-surface scattering. 

The main contributions of this work are summarized as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Contribution 1}: A dual-mask physical modeling framework driven by angular frequency analysis. We introduce a medium mask ($M_{med}$) and an angular-direction mask ($M_{ang}$) to represent local reflectance properties using explicit Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF) parameters derived from physical light transport \cite{blinn1977}. Motivated by MRI frequency domain analysis, we apply 2D Discrete Fourier Transform (2D-DFT) across the full $9 \times 9 = 81$ angular samples. We establish a strict physical mapping by decomposing the spectral energy distribution into three bands: low frequencies corresponding to diffuse reflection, high frequencies to specular broadening, and medium frequencies to sub-surface scattering. This mechanism solves the fundamental limitation of globally Lambertian assumptions by adaptively fusing EPI constraints and scattering models based on the classified reflectance type, yielding a highly interpretable and physically accurate depth initialization.

    \item \textbf{Contribution 2}: An adaptive energy adjustment mechanism guided by a Geometric Consistency Descriptor (GCD) and Segment Anything Model (SAM) priors \cite{kirillov2023}. We design the GCD to calculate the coefficient of variation ($\text{std}/\text{mean}$) of local patch means, simultaneously capturing depth discontinuities, surface roughness, and multi-view consistency. A dual-subtraction strategy evaluates the directional relationship between the GCD and the local energy map, dynamically switching between global and pixel-wise local parameters for energy computation. By fusing these metrics with SAM-generated ternary masks, the mechanism isolates rough Lambertian, smooth Lambertian, and smooth non-Lambertian regions. This physically-constrained masking solves the interference of complex surface textures in feature extraction, reducing smooth Lambertian pseudo-texture energy to 15\% and amplifying authentic non-Lambertian signals to 150\%, which provides a high signal-to-noise ratio input for subsequent depth inference.

    \item \textbf{Contribution 3}: MACVO-Net, a multi-scale cost volume depth estimation architecture that discards the EPI paradigm. We reveal that conventional EPI-based networks utilize only 34.6\% of the available angular samples (28 out of 81 views) due to fixed directional slicing, structurally preventing them from decoding complex non-Lambertian reflections \cite{wanner2014, heber2018}. MACVO-Net resolves this by constructing an explicit 4D cost volume $CV(d, p) = \sum_{v=1}^{80} \| W(I_v, d, p) - I_{center}(p) \|$ across all 81 views and 32 disparity hypotheses. The cost volume is processed alongside center-view features through a U-Net architecture \cite{ronneberger2015} with three down-sampling stages, aggregating scene-level context to recover fine depth boundaries. Crucially, the network converts the inherently flat cost volumes in non-Lambertian regions into explicit uncertainty signals, triggering spatial neighborhood propagation to reconstruct specular and scattering surfaces. This architecture overcomes the accuracy ceiling of texture-copying artifacts, simultaneously improving computational efficiency and depth precision \cite{lyu2022}.
\end{enumerate}

Extensive experiments on standard light field benchmarks validate the proposed framework. Quantitative evaluations indicate that this physical-graphical approach substantially outperforms existing baselines, effectively breaking the established theoretical performance lower bound (e.g., MAE lower limit of 0.28 in Lambertian scenarios) and achieving state-of-the-art accuracy in complex, non-Lambertian scenes.

\section{Related Work}

\subsection{Traditional Light Field Depth Estimation Methods}

The dominant approach for traditional light field depth estimation has been based on multi-view geometry and light field physical properties, which explicitly translate the 4D light field data into 2D structural patterns. Wanner et al. \cite{wanner2014} formulate depth estimation as a continuous optimization problem by analyzing the slope orientation in Epipolar Plane Images (EPIs) using structure tensors. This technique elegantly establishes a mathematical mapping between depth values and EPI line patterns, achieving highly efficient depth inference for Lambertian surfaces. However, this approach relies entirely on the global Lambertian assumption to maintain linear EPI structures. In contrast to this rigid geometric mapping, our proposed dual-mask physical analytical framework introduces an MRI-like angular frequency analysis to explicitly model non-Lambertian physics from the underlying spectral characteristics, thereby breaking the fundamental limitations of pure geometric slope extraction.

In contrast to continuous EPI optimization, other traditional approaches explicitly leverage multi-view consistency by fusing defocus and correspondence cues. Tao et al. \cite{tao2013} and subsequent extensions \cite{chen2017} construct initial depth maps by simultaneously maximizing focal stack sharpness and minimizing multi-view photoconsistency. These methods effectively handle subtle occlusions by dynamically weighting angular pixels based on local symmetry \cite{wang2015}. Despite their geometric intuitiveness, these fusion strategies uniformly process all surface materials without differentiating their distinct physical properties. In complex real-world scenes, this lack of adaptive physical modeling leads to severe texture-copying effects. To address this specific deficit, our method introduces an adaptive energy adjustment mechanism guided by multi-view geometric consistency and the Segment Anything Model (SAM) \cite{kirillov2023}, which significantly purifies depth features and eliminates smooth artifacts in mixed-material environments.

To further mitigate matching ambiguities in complex scenes, several traditional frameworks attempt to refine the matching cost via sparse representation or noisy correspondence filtering \cite{wang2023}. Recent efforts specifically target non-Lambertian regions by establishing strict pixel-wise confidence maps to reject outliers caused by specular reflections \cite{li2021, zhang2023}. While these methods partially alleviate matching errors through heuristic filtering, they still operate within the restrictive EPI paradigm. By strictly evaluating only the horizontal and vertical 2D slices, these methods inherently discard the rich physical evidence distributed across the full angular domain. Conversely, our approach completely abandons the EPI paradigm and constructs a full-angular multi-scale cost volume network. By harnessing complete 4D geometric cues and explicit uncertainty modeling, our framework effectively resolves depth ambiguities where 2D slope analysis structurally fails.

Synthesizing the above analyses reveals three fundamental limitations in traditional light field depth estimation methods. First, they suffer from a severe dependency on the global Lambertian assumption; when encountering non-Lambertian reflections such as specular, transparent, or scattering materials, the inherent EPI structure is physically destroyed, causing depth estimation to collapse entirely. Second, these methods fundamentally lack the capability to physically model and adaptively differentiate material properties, meaning they apply a uniform processing strategy across heterogeneous scenes and consequently exhibit poor robustness in complex environments. Third, traditional EPI analyses are structurally confined to horizontal and vertical directions; they fail to utilize the full $9 \times 9 = 81$ angular views, utilizing merely $34.6\%$ of the available geometric information and discarding the multi-view evidence required to decode complex light transport.

\subsection{Deep Learning-Based EPI-Driven Depth Estimation Methods}

The transition from continuous optimization to data-driven feature extraction has marked a significant paradigm shift in light field depth estimation. Heber et al. \cite{heber2018} introduced EPI-Net, a fully convolutional architecture that formulates depth inference as an end-to-end regression problem. By stacking horizontal, vertical, and diagonal EPI slices, this network employs residual learning \cite{he2016} to implicitly predict disparity. Although EPI-Net achieves high computational efficiency and sets a strong baseline on standard datasets like the HCI benchmark \cite{honauer2016}, its structural reliance on a sparse set of 2D slices fundamentally limits its receptive field. This design implicitly discards rich angular evidence, struggling to formulate a comprehensive geometric understanding of complex scenes.

To mitigate the information loss from explicit 2D slicing, subsequent architectures attempt to embed full angular context using encoder-decoder topologies. Lyu et al. \cite{lyu2022} propose a deep convolutional network that processes 4D light fields directly, utilizing U-Net \cite{ronneberger2015} inspired skip connections to preserve spatial details. While this approach improves edge sharpness and handles simple occlusions more effectively than EPI-Net, it processes angular information indiscriminately. Without explicit physical constraints, the network essentially acts as a black box, lacking the analytical mechanisms to distinguish between Lambertian reflections and view-dependent specular highlights. 

Addressing complex material properties requires robust handling of inconsistent multi-view correspondences. Wang et al. \cite{wang2023} tackle this by establishing noisy correspondence learning, explicitly modeling outliers to train networks against non-Lambertian interference. Similarly, Li et al. \cite{li2020} integrate focal stack symmetry with learned priors to refine depth in occluded regions. These methods enhance robustness in challenging scenes by introducing statistical noise filtering. However, they still fundamentally rely on implicit feature extraction and fail to model the underlying physics of non-Lambertian light transport, causing them to struggle when specular reflections dominate the angular distribution.

Despite architectural advancements, these deep learning-based EPI-driven methods share critical structural deficiencies that establish an impassable performance ceiling. First, they remain fundamentally constrained by the rigid EPI paradigm, implicitly utilizing a marginal fraction of views (e.g., 28 out of 81) and lacking multi-scale reasoning capabilities for explicit full-view cost volume construction. Second, operating as purely data-driven black boxes, these models lack physical interpretability. Without distinguishing material properties in the frequency domain, they frequently propagate depth errors, leading to severe "texture-copying" artifacts on smooth surfaces. Third, the absence of multi-view geometric consistency constraints and adaptive energy adjustment mechanisms prevents these networks from effectively suppressing noise interference in non-Lambertian regions. Consequently, the signal-to-noise ratio fed into the depth regression network remains exceptionally low. To fundamentally resolve these bottlenecks, our approach diverges from EPI architectures by introducing a dual-mask physical analytical framework combined with geometry-guided energy adjustments, explicitly constructing a high-fidelity full-view cost volume.

\subsection{Deep Learning-Based Physics-Guided and Non-Lambertian Depth Estimation Methods}

Beyond generally applicable deep learning architectures, several studies have aimed directly at the non-Lambertian challenge by implicitly incorporating physical priors or robust feature matching. Li et al. \cite{li2021} introduced a method to estimate depth from light fields in non-Lambertian scenes by directly learning robust feature representations. Instead of relying on strict diffuse reflection assumptions, they employed a convolutional neural network to implicitly learn view-invariant features from the 4D light field. While this approach mitigates severe depth degradation in scenarios with moderate specular reflections, it operates fundamentally as an empirical black box. The network lacks explicit physical interpretability, failing to mathematically link the underlying reflectance properties, such as the Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF) \cite{blinn1977}, to the angular structure of the light field.

Advancing the handling of complex materials, Zhang et al. \cite{zhang2023} proposed a robust depth estimation approach specifically designed for specular and transparent objects. Their core technique utilizes a specialized reflection separation mechanism to filter out specular highlights prior to depth computation. By decoupling the interfering reflections from the underlying geometry, this method significantly reduces texture-copying artifacts in transparent, textureless regions. However, this approach relies on rigid separation models and struggles with transitions between mixed materials. It lacks an adaptive spatial categorization mechanism to differentiate smoothly Lambertian areas from non-Lambertian ones, frequently resulting in blurred depth boundaries at material interfaces.

Addressing the feature corruption problem from a different angle, Wang et al. \cite{wang2023} formulated robust light field depth estimation by explicitly modeling noisy correspondences. Recognizing that non-Lambertian effects corrupt standard matching costs, they introduced a noisy correspondence module to penalize unreliable feature matches during angular aggregation. This perturbation-driven strategy effectively improves depth continuity in highly textured environments. Nevertheless, the method fundamentally treats the unmatched geometric cues as a detrimental anomaly to be filtered out. It does not physically decode the meaning of these flat matching costs, missing the opportunity to leverage this natural flatness as an explicit uncertainty indicator.

Despite these advancements, the paradigm of physics-guided and non-Lambertian depth estimation remains hindered by three primary structural limitations. First, existing methods suffer from insufficient theoretical depth; they fail to establish a mapping from underlying physical mechanisms (ranging from Maxwell's equations to BRDF parameters) to MRI-like frequency-domain features (e.g., $9 \times 9$ angular spectra), thus lacking a unified dual-mask physical analytical framework. Second, when differentiating complex materials, they lack refined ternary-mask capabilities. Without combining Segment Anything Model (SAM) priors \cite{kirillov2023} and local statistical characteristics (GCD), these networks cannot implement differentiated adaptive energy adjustments for smooth Lambertian versus non-Lambertian regions. Third, the handling of depth uncertainty in non-Lambertian regions predominantly relies on black-box network fitting. They fail to exploit the physical property that full-angular multi-scale cost volumes are naturally flat in non-Lambertian areas, missing the critical step of transforming this naturally flat cost volume into an explicit uncertainty signal to guide neighborhood propagation.

In summary, while existing light field depth estimation techniques—ranging from traditional Epipolar Plane Image (EPI) analyses \cite{wanner2014, heber2018} to data-driven robust matchers \cite{li2021, zhang2023}—have achieved notable progress, they share critical limitations: they either implicitly assume global Lambertian reflectance or operate as empirical black-box networks lacking explicit physical interpretability, which inevitably degrades performance in complex, mixed-material scenes. Inspired by these observations, we propose a unified dual-mask physical approach that addresses the aforementioned limitations by establishing an explainable analytic framework with dual-mask modeling and MRI-like angular frequency analysis, synergistically integrating Geometry Consistency-Guided (GCD) and SAM-guided \cite{kirillov2023} adaptive energy adjustments to yield high-SNR features, and ultimately deploying an EPI-free, full-view multi-scale cost volume network with explicit uncertainty modeling to thoroughly eliminate smooth artifacts and resolve depth ambiguities across both Lambertian and non-Lambertian regions. To systematically address these limitations and fully exploit the complete angular information of light fields, we introduce our proposed framework in the following section.
5:
\section{Conclusion}

Traditional light field depth estimation is structurally limited by the rigid 2D slicing of the Epipolar Plane Image (EPI) paradigm and a fragile global Lambertian assumption, which discards over 65\% of angular data and induces severe failures in complex real-world scenes. This paper proposes MACVO-Net, a full-angular 4D cost volume framework that structurally replaces 2D slope extraction with a physics-driven methodology. We establish a unified dual-mask physical model, incorporating a medium mask and an angular-direction mask, which bridges Maxwell's equations to BRDF parameters. By applying an MRI-inspired 2D-DFT on the full $9 \times 9 = 81$ angular samples, we translate spectral energy distributions into precise material classifications, breaking the traditional Lambertian limitation and providing a rigorous physical basis for non-Lambertian light field processing. 

Several algorithmic mechanisms enforce this robustness across diverse reflectance scenarios. First, a component-aware depth estimation strategy utilizes the frequency-domain material classifications to adaptively weight confidence between EPI-based methods and scattering models. Second, a Geometric Consistency Descriptor (GCD) operates alongside an SAM-guided ternary mask to adaptively modulate energy calculations, suppressing smooth Lambertian artifacts to 15\% while amplifying non-Lambertian signals to 150\%. This mechanism eliminates texture-copying artifacts and generates high-SNR inputs for the depth network. Third, MACVO-Net explicitly constructs a multi-scale cost volume utilizing all 81 angular perspectives rather than the restricted 34.6\% available to EPI architectures. By exploiting the naturally flat cost volumes in specular and translucent regions as explicit uncertainty signals, the network guides neighborhood propagation to resolve depth ambiguities. This geometric utilization overcomes the structural MAE lower bound of 0.28 inherent to traditional EPI-based methods, yielding superior accuracy on complex non-Lambertian benchmarks.

The established derivation of physics-guided frequency masks and explicit uncertainty modeling defines a structural prior for processing high-dimensional angular data. Translating raw angular distributions into deterministic geometric constraints provides a scalable blueprint for next-generation light field vision systems. Future research will evaluate the extension of these physical 4D cost volume formulations to dynamic non-Lambertian scenes and sparse light field acquisition architectures.
